svg.text: mkdir(p) · ls(p) · delete(p) · move(s, d) · copy(s, d)
every method takes a PATH — resolver turns it into a Node

g.text: File AND Directory are both a Node.
EVER asking “is this a file?” — that
is the Composite pattern in one line.

svg.text: delegates every op to PathResolver + the tree
guards the tree with one ReadWriteLock
getPath() — walks parent chain to root
isolated + unit-tested — the trickiest bit of the design
split on '/', then for each segment:
absolute starts from root, relative from cwd · O(d) in depth
reads dominate a filesystem → RWLock beats a
plain mutex: browsing never blocks browsing.
move() locks BOTH parents in ID order → no deadlock
at scale: per-node lock instead of one global lock
InMemoryFileSystem — two ways to store the tree
getPath() walks parents · no path strings stored
move = re-point ONE pointer (paths are derived)
lookup O(d) · shared prefixes → low memory
start here in the room — it shows the pattern
lookup O(1) by full path — no traversal
stores full path strings → more memory
mention as the high-throughput random-access tweak
recursive search (find): DFS / BFS over the Composite — predicate on name / size / mtime, glob *.txt on filename
rmdir refuses a non-empty dir (DirectoryNotEmptyException) · every write bumps modifiedAt · SymLink = a new leaf, zero core edits

g.text: RECURSIVE SIZE — one method, no instanceof:
polymorphism does the branching for you —
O(n) over the subtree, O(h) stack depth.

svg.text: SymLink = new leaf holding a target path; resolve()
follows it, cap depth ~40 + visited-set for cycles.
Persistence: snapshot the tree + write-ahead log,
replay on restart (how ext4 / NTFS journals work).
① the API (paths in) → ② Composite Node tree (File leaf / Directory composite) → ③ PathResolver normalizes . and .. → ④ ReadWriteLock guards it → ⑤ tree vs flat-map trade-off

g.text: Patterns: Composite · Template Method · Strategy · ReadWriteLock
In-memory Unix-like file system — machine-coding LLD
